agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposed reduction of delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days across a logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets, serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who rely on the data for their own compliance reporting.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Record Lifecycle / Data Retention & Compliance",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete dataset capturing a delivery transaction: proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient details, item descriptions, customs declarations, pricing, and chain-of-custody events." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The rules governing how long delivery records are stored before archival or deletion, driven by legal, regulatory, contractual, and operational requirements." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient — signatures, photos, GPS coordinates. Required for dispute resolution and insurance claims." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The process by which business customers extract delivery data from the platform to satisfy their own tax, customs, and audit obligations." },
      { "term": "Limitation Period", "definition": "The legal window during which a party may bring a claim related to a delivery — typically 6 years in England/Wales, varying across EU member states." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records containing tax-relevant data (VAT, customs declarations, commercial invoices) must be retained for the minimum period required by the applicable tax authority — 6 years for UK HMRC, 5-10 years depending on EU member state.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal obligation, not a policy preference. Violation exposes the company to regulatory penalties, failed audits, and potential criminal liability for destruction of tax records.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record containing tax-relevant fields is deleted before the applicable statutory retention period has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Proof of Delivery artifacts must be retained for the full limitation period during which a customer or recipient may bring a contractual or tortious claim — 6 years in England/Wales (Limitation Act 1980), varying by EU jurisdiction.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Destroying POD evidence before the limitation period expires removes the company's ability to defend against delivery disputes, chargebacks, and insurance claims. This is a legal and financial integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "No Proof of Delivery record is deleted before the applicable limitation period has expired from the delivery date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires personal data to be kept no longer than necessary for the purposes for which it is processed — but 'necessary' includes legal obligations, legitimate interests, and contractual commitments, not merely operational convenience.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR compliance is a legal invariant, but it does NOT mandate 90-day deletion. The lawful basis for retention (legal obligation under tax law, contract performance for B2B customers) justifies the current 7-year window. Premature deletion could actually violate other legal obligations.",
      "testable_assertion": "The retention period for each delivery record category is justified by a documented lawful basis under GDPR Article 6, and no record is retained beyond its justified period OR deleted before its legally required minimum period."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "B2B customers who contractually rely on the platform for compliance data access must be given sufficient notice and transition time before any reduction in data availability, per the terms of their service agreements.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a contractual and commercial obligation that could theoretically be renegotiated, but unilateral changes would breach existing contracts and destroy customer trust.",
      "testable_assertion": "No B2B customer loses access to delivery records they are contractually entitled to access without explicit written agreement and a transition period."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Cross-border delivery records involving customs declarations must be retained per the Union Customs Code (Regulation EU 952/2013) — minimum 3 years from end of the year in which the customs declaration was accepted, but member states may extend this.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Customs records retention is a statutory obligation enforced by customs authorities. Destruction of these records can result in fines, loss of Authorised Economic Operator status, and inability to clear future shipments.",
      "testable_assertion": "No customs-related delivery record is deleted before the applicable customs retention period has elapsed."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer requests delivery records from 4 months ago for a tax audit by their national authority. Under the 90-day policy, the data has been destroyed.", "expected_behavior": "This must never happen. The platform is contractually and often legally obligated to provide this data. The B2B customer faces regulatory penalties due to our infrastructure decision." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer files a chargeback or delivery dispute 5 months after delivery. Under the 90-day policy, the Proof of Delivery has been destroyed.", "expected_behavior": "The company cannot defend the chargeback and absorbs the financial loss. At scale, this creates a systematic inability to contest fraudulent claims." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC or an EU tax authority audits the company and requests VAT records for deliveries from 2 years ago.", "expected_behavior": "The company must produce these records. Under the 90-day policy, they have been destroyed, resulting in regulatory penalties and potential criminal liability." },
    { "scenario": "A cross-border delivery is flagged in a customs investigation 18 months after the fact. The customs declaration records have been deleted.", "expected_behavior": "Loss of Authorised Economic Operator status, fines, and potential suspension of customs clearance privileges." },
    { "scenario": "A personal injury claim is filed 3 years after a delivery incident. Delivery route, driver assignment, and timing records have been deleted.", "expected_behavior": "The company loses its ability to reconstruct events for legal defense. Liability exposure increases dramatically." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Storage cost savings are marginal compared to: (1) regulatory fines — GDPR fines up to 4% global revenue, HMRC penalties for missing tax records, customs penalties; (2) inability to contest chargebacks and delivery disputes, creating a systematic revenue leak; (3) potential breach-of-contract claims from B2B customers; (4) loss of insurance defense capability for delivery-related claims.",
    "operational": "DESTRUCTIVE. B2B customers depend on historical delivery data for their own compliance. Destroying this data forces them to build parallel record-keeping systems or switch providers. The platform's value proposition as a 'system of record' for logistics operations is fundamentally undermined.",
    "user": "B2C consumers lose the ability to reference past deliveries for disputes, returns, or warranty claims after 90 days. B2B customers lose access to compliance-critical data, potentially putting them in regulatory jeopardy. Trust in the platform as a reliable logistics partner is severely damaged.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL. This change simultaneously violates multiple legal retention requirements (UK tax law, EU customs regulations, limitation periods for civil claims), breaches B2B contractual obligations, and paradoxically does NOT satisfy GDPR better — since GDPR permits retention where there is a lawful basis, which exists here. The framing as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' is dangerously incorrect.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Loss of compliance data access; potential breach of their own regulatory obligations; likely contract breach triggering SLA penalties or termination rights" },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "Loss of delivery history for disputes, chargebacks, warranty claims, and personal records beyond 90 days" },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax Team", "impact": "Inability to produce records for VAT audits; direct regulatory exposure with HMRC and EU tax authorities" },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance Team", "impact": "Loss of evidence for defending disputes, chargebacks, personal injury claims, and contractual claims within the limitation period" },
      { "group": "Customs / Trade Compliance", "impact": "Inability to produce customs declarations for audits; risk to Authorised Economic Operator status" },
      { "group": "Insurance / Risk Management", "impact": "Loss of delivery records needed to file or defend insurance claims" },
      { "group": "Engineering / Infrastructure", "impact": "The stated beneficiary — but storage cost reduction can be achieved through tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) without destroying legally required data" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "reasoning": "The regulatory landscape for logistics data retention in EU/UK is well-established and unambiguous. UK HMRC 6-year retention, EU customs retention obligations, and civil limitation periods are settled law. The business impact is clear and severe. The only uncertainty is the specific contractual terms with B2B customers, which could affect the magnitude but not the direction of impact.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK tax record retention requirements (6 years, HMRC)",
      "EU customs record retention requirements (Union Customs Code)",
      "Civil limitation periods for delivery disputes (6 years England/Wales)",
      "GDPR does not mandate 90-day deletion when lawful basis exists for longer retention",
      "B2B customers will be severely impacted"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact contractual terms with specific B2B customers",
      "Specific EU member state variations in retention periods beyond the baseline",
      "Current storage cost breakdown (the actual savings may be trivial compared to risk)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform processes VAT-relevant transactions",
      "Cross-border deliveries involve customs declarations",
      "B2B customer contracts include data access provisions",
      "The platform operates as a data controller or joint controller under GDPR"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The request frames this as 'infrastructure optimization — no business logic changes,' but reducing retention from 7 years to 90 days directly violates multiple statutory retention requirements (UK HMRC 6-year tax records, EU customs retention, civil limitation periods). This is not an infrastructure change — it is a compliance-critical business rule change that has been mischaracterized. The 7-year retention period exists FOR legal reasons, not by accident.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Finance/Tax", "B2B Customers", "Customs/Trade"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day deletion proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B: Implement tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) to reduce costs while preserving all legally required data — move records older than 90 days to cold storage",
        "C: Conduct a formal Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and legal review to define per-record-type retention schedules that satisfy all statutory minimums"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B for immediate cost relief, followed by Option C for a legally sound, optimized retention policy. Option A (the status quo) is the safe default until B and C are completed. Under NO circumstances should 90-day auto-deletion proceed."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days destroys tax records before the statutory 6-year HMRC retention period", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days destroys Proof of Delivery before the 6-year civil limitation period expires", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "The change does not satisfy GDPR better — GDPR permits retention where lawful basis exists, and premature deletion violates other legal obligations", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "B2B customers contractually relying on delivery data for compliance reporting would lose access without consent or transition", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "Cross-border customs records would be destroyed before the Union Customs Code retention period expires", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" }
  ]
}
